comments:
This manuscript describes an effort to develop a Tree Adjoining Grammar (TAG) for Tamil, an in-house TAG chart parser implementation, and the use of TAG derivation trees to extract dependency/semantic relations (nominal subject, direct object, root) for Tamil sentences. The topic is relevant: applying TAGs to morphologically rich Indian languages and exploiting derivation structure for dependency extraction is interesting and potentially useful. The authors report a hand-built grammar (≈120 trees: 25 initial, 95 auxiliary), a multithreaded Java implementation of an Earley-type TAG parser, and some qualitative example parses. They state they preserve all ambiguous parses rather than ranking them and map dependency output to a Stanford-like dependency set.

Major concerns and weaknesses:
- Lack of evaluation: there is no quantitative experiment or evaluation (no gold-standard data, no precision/recall or attachment scores) demonstrating that the approach yields correct dependencies for Tamil. Claims that derivations “mine semantics” are not substantiated by measurements. Without empirical results the contribution is merely descriptive and preliminary.
- Insufficient detail on resources and coverage: the paper gives only a high-level count of trees but no inventory of coverage (which constructions are supported/unsupported), no corpora description, and no dataset of test sentences. The stated domain (tourism and health) and “textbook class sentences” suggests limited scope; this should be made explicit and measured.
- Missing figures and examples: the text references many figures (derivation trees, elementary trees, parser output) but none are present in the provided text. Concrete, fully worked Tamil examples (with Romanization and glosses) and the exact derived dependency triples would make the claims verifiable.
- Morphology handling: Tamil is agglutinative and the paper explicitly avoids morphological analysis. This is a major limitation because dependencies in Tamil are often realized morphologically (case, agreement). The authors must justify or address how ignoring morphology affects correctness; better would be to integrate or at least pre-process morphology and show the effect.
- Parser and algorithmic details are sparse: the implementation is said to be an Earley-type TAG parser (Schabes & Joshi), multithreaded Java, pseudo-lexicalized; however, complexity, pruning strategies, how ambiguities are represented and returned, memory/time performance, and handling of anchoring (they mention single anchor per tree) are not described.
- Weak comparison to prior work: the paper cites foundational TAG literature and mentions Joshi & Rambow, XTAG, and synchronous TAGs, but lacks a focused comparison to existing dependency extraction or parsing work on Tamil (or other morph-rich languages), and to established dependency parsers. The decision to keep all ambiguous parses instead of ranking should be justified and discussed relative to practical use.
- Presentation quality and writing: the manuscript has many typographical errors, repeated sentences, and informal phrasing; it frequently references figures and examples that are absent. The exposition needs reorganization: separate sections for grammar design, parser implementation, examples, and evaluation would help.

Suggestions for improvement:
1) Add a clear evaluation: create or use an annotated Tamil dependency/dev/test set and report standard metrics (UAS/LAS, precision/recall on nsub/dobj/root) comparing dependency extraction from TAG derivations to a baseline (e.g., a statistical dependency parser or rule-based system). Provide both qualitative error analysis and quantitative numbers.
2) Include full example parses with derivation trees, the exact mapping rules from derivation nodes to dependency relations, and demonstrate how morphological information (or lack thereof) affects outputs. Show multiple ambiguous parses and how each maps to different dependency interpretations.
3) Describe the grammar in more detail: sample elementary trees, anchoring policy, treatment of postpositions/case markers, verb classes (transitive/intransitive/ergative), and which Tamil constructions are covered or intentionally excluded.
4) Provide parser implementation details: complexity, pruning strategies, how multiple parses are stored/returned, runtime and memory on example inputs, and whether the parser is robust to noisy text.
5) Improve literature comparison: discuss related work on Tamil parsing (statistical and rule-based), other TAG-based dependency efforts, and explain advantages/disadvantages of the proposed method.
6) Clean up writing and include all referenced figures and tables.
7) If possible, release the grammar and parser or a sample dataset so others can reproduce and build upon the work.

Overall assessment: the work is an early-stage, descriptive study that explores a potentially useful application of TAGs to Tamil dependency extraction, but in its current form it is underspecified and unsupported by empirical evidence. The manuscript would be appropriate for publication after substantial revision adding concrete examples, experiments, and clearer presentation.
